coredata.dc:title: A pH-responsive sensor based on intramolecular internal standard for reproducible detection of strong acids and bases via 19F NMR spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Jang, Mincheol
Han, Min Su
coredata.dc:description: Numerous methods, including pH meters and optical sensors, have been developed for the detection of pH, which is an important indicator in various fields. However, those methods are susceptible to errors in strongly acidic and basic ranges and inaccurate pH measurement due to sample turbidity, hindering their application such as photographic industries and wastewater treatment facilities. Eco-friendly and non-invasive 19F NMR spectroscopy is a promising technique for measurement of strong acids and bases owing to its high sensitivity and little interference; nevertheless, inconsistencies in reproducibility impede its widespread adoption. Herein, we developed a19F NMR-based pH sensor by introducing an intramolecular internal standard strategy into a pH-responsive fluorinated material. Based on the acceptable deviation (ΔδF = 17−19 ppb) in the evaluation of the internal standard signal, this pH-sensing platform enabled reproducible pH measurements in strongly acidic and basic environments. Moreover, its 19F NMR response showed reversibility and high stability to potential interfering factors, and the low absolute difference (0.026−0.086 in pH) for real samples such as diet Coke suggests its potential suitability for various acidic beverages.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Internal standard
Reproducibility
19F NMR spectroscopy
pH detection
Real samples

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of 19F pH sensor 1 for pH detection
Design and synthesis of pH sensor 1 based on intramolecular internal standard
Validation of the intramolecular internal standard in 19F NMR spectroscopy
pH titration of pH sensor 1 via 19F NMR spectroscopy
Interference and reversibility test of pH sensor 1 via 19F NMR spectroscopy
Applications in acidic beverages via 19F NMR spectroscopy

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Secondary screening experiments of two optimized candidates (2-amino-5-fluorophenol and 3-amino-4-fluorophenol, 10 mM) through pH titration in the pH range from 1.0 to 13.0 via 19F NMR spectroscopy. (A) 19F NMR spectra of each fluorinated derivative with TFA. The fluorine signal intensity of each measured sample in the range of −109.0 to −154.0 ppm was magnified a) 2 times and b) 7 times in comparison to that of the TFA signal intensity. Plot of chemical shift (δF) based on TFA (−76.550 ppm) of (B) 2-amino-5-fluorophenol and (C) 3-amino-4-fluorophenol, versus pH.

pH titration experiment of pH sensor 1 (5 mM) in the pH range from 1.0 to 13.0 via 19F NMR spectroscopy. All FR signal intensities in the range of −69.4 to −86.4 ppm were magnified 5 to 7 times vs the FI signal intensity.
Intramolecular internal standard strategy derived by pH sensor 1-based reproducible pH detection in strongly acidic and basic conditions using 19F NMR spectroscopy.

Comparative analysis across institutions with various NMR frequencies. pH sensor 1 at (A) pH 1.0 and (B) pH 13.0. Institutions: a) JEOL JNM-ECS400, b) BRUKER AVANCE III HD Nanobay 400, c) BRUKER AVANCE NEO Nanobay 400, d) BRUKER AVANCE NEO 500, and e) AGILENT 400 MHz 54 mm NMR DD2.
Interference test of pH sensor 1 using 19F NMR spectroscopy through pH measurements in multi-ionic samples.

ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: AD: Absolute difference (AD) between calculated pH and adjusted pH.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: pH determination of real samples using the pH meter and pH sensor 1 based on 19F NMR spectroscopy.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: AD: Absolute difference (AD) between calculated pH and pH measured by pH meter.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: RSD: Abbreviation of relative standard deviation.

head.ce:title: A pH-responsive sensor based on intramolecular internal standard for reproducible detection of strong acids and bases via 19F NMR spectroscopy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology, 123 Cheomdangwagi-ro, Buk-gu, Gwangju, 61005, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Gwangju Institute of Science and Technology, 123 Cheomdangwagi-ro, Buk-gu, Gwangju 61005, Republic of Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Numerous methods, including pH meters and optical sensors, have been developed for the detection of pH, which is an important indicator in various fields. However, those methods are susceptible to errors in strongly acidic and basic ranges and inaccurate pH measurement due to sample turbidity, hindering their application such as photographic industries and wastewater treatment facilities. Eco-friendly and non-invasive 19F NMR spectroscopy is a promising technique for measurement of strong acids and bases owing to its high sensitivity and little interference; nevertheless, inconsistencies in reproducibility impede its widespread adoption. Herein, we developed a19F NMR-based pH sensor by introducing an intramolecular internal standard strategy into a pH-responsive fluorinated material. Based on the acceptable deviation (ΔδF = 17−19 ppb) in the evaluation of the internal standard signal, this pH-sensing platform enabled reproducible pH measurements in strongly acidic and basic environments. Moreover, its 19F NMR response showed reversibility and high stability to potential interfering factors, and the low absolute difference (0.026−0.086 in pH) for real samples such as diet Coke suggests its potential suitability for various acidic beverages.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: pH is a critical parameter that spans a wide range, from strongly acidic to strongly basic, making it a versatile indicator utilized in various fields, including medical diagnostics and the chemical and beverage industries [1–6]. Deviations from the normal pH range can have serious consequences, leading to disease or destruction of plant and animal life [7–9]. Therefore, accurate pH detection and measurement are essential to ensure optimal performance and safety. To date, several methods have been reported for pH detection, largely based around pH meters and optical pH sensors [10–12]. Although pH meters are known for their ease of use and high accuracy, they are prone to sample loss and contamination [7], and their reliability can be compromised at both ends of the pH range [13]. Additionally, the glass electrode used in pH meters is fragile and requires an internal solution as a reference for accurate measurement [14,15]. Optical pH sensors, on the other hand, detect pH changes through the absorbance or luminescence of a specific indicator molecule [16–23]. While they offer high sensitivity and fast response, most optical sensors suffer from operational instability, necessitating additional calibration [24], or can only detect a narrow pH range [25]. Furthermore, the response of optical sensors can be affected by the sample's state, making accurate measurement difficult for opaque samples with dark colors that interfere with their photophysical properties [26]. In other words, these methods can be unsuitable for pH detection in strongly acidic and basic environments, including certain applications such as the photographic and dye industries and wastewater treatment facilities [13]. Hence, the development of new system capable of accurately measuring pH in extreme environments holds great significance.

Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR) spectroscopy is an uncomplicated and eco-friendly technique that has been widely utilized to determine the pH of samples with high precision and excellent reproducibility [7]. Molecules with functional groups that are sensitive to the environment at a specific pH provide a diversity of chemical shifts in response to changes and can be used as NMR indicators [27–29]. For example, the use of 1D NMR of various nuclei, such as 1H, 19F, and 31P, has been reported for pH determination [30–32]. However, the range of measurement by 1H NMR indicators is narrow, resulting in small chemical shift differences even under extremely acidic and basic conditions [7], and also can be obscured by background signals. Furthermore, despite the fact that phosphorylated derivatives with low toxicity and a broad range pK a values have been widely used as pH sensors using 31P NMR spectroscopy, it necessitates the synthesis of materials with various structures as each structure is only suitable for a particular pH range [33–35]. In contrast, the 19F NMR spectroscopy is highly sensitive (83% of 1H), enabling subtle changes in the local environment to be detected and providing a wide measurement range [36]. Furthermore, the 19F nucleus is rarely present in biological samples, resulting in little interference in detection [37]. Based on these features, 19F NMR-based research has seen a surge in applications in recent years [38]. However, heteronuclear NMR spectroscopy, such as 19F NMR, has not yet been optimized for current reference protocols, and improvements in reproducibility are essential [38].

The establishment of a system that can precisely determine the relationship between chemical shift and pH may result in the creation of a reliable pH sensor based on NMR spectroscopy [7]. From this perspective, strategies that utilize standard signals that yield a constant result for a measured value through a particular signal are commonly employed to improve reproducibility. Although standard signals can be generated using external standard materials, this approach reduces the efficiency in terms of the sensor development. Conversely, the use of an internal standard strategy that relies on a specific internal signal as the standard signal allows for accurate analysis through self-calibration and eliminates the need for additional reference materials [39,40]. This strategy is advantageous for the development of simple sensor systems with minimal components, and has recently attracted the attention of researchers because of accuracy of the results. For instance, some researchers have attempted to develop ratiometric optical sensors based on a single probe by combining two or more dyes or by synthesizing a single intrinsic dual-emissive nanoprobe [41–44]. Recently, our group successfully detected various diols by 19F NMR spectroscopy based on an intramolecular internal standard, demonstrating improved reproducibility using this strategy [45]. However, there are very few examples of this efficient strategy being applied to the development of 19F NMR-based sensors.

In this study, pH sensor 1 using 19F NMR spectroscopy was developed based on an intramolecular internal standard strategy. The sensor utilizes the chemical shift difference between the pH-sensitive fluorine signal and the other fluorine signal, maintaining a constant chemical shift despite pH changes in the surrounding environment (Scheme 1 ). To this end, a fluorinated compound with both amino and hydroxyl moieties as pH-responsive groups was selected to measure pH under strongly acidic and basic conditions. Then, the trifluoromethyl moiety, as the internal standard, was covalently introduced through a simple one-step reaction. The aromatic fluorine signal of the sensor demonstrated a shift in chemical shift upon protonation and deprotonation, whereas the internal standard signal was expected to remain constant. With improved reproducibility owing to the internal standard signal, the sensor accurately measured pH in a simple yet strongly acidic and basic range, without the need for an external standard as an additive. Furthermore, the sensor was tested for interference from various ions such as metal ions, and its potential application in real samples such as Coke was investigated.

All chemical reagents were purchased from commercial suppliers (Sigma-Aldrich, Alfa Aesar, Tokyo Chemical Industry, Duksan Pure Chemicals, Samchun Chemicals, and Combi-Blocks) and used without further purification. Different brands of beverages (diet Coke and lemonade) were purchased from a local convenience store. All the chemical shifts of the synthesized compounds were assigned and recorded on a JEOL 400 MHz NMR spectrometer operating at 400 MHz for 1H NMR, 101 MHz for 13C NMR, and 376 MHz for 19F NMR. High-resolution mass spectra (HRMS) were collected at the Korea Basic Science Institute (Daegu, South Korea) using a JEOL JMS-700 MStation mass spectrometer equipped with a standard electron ionization (EI+) source. The pH measurements of real samples were performed using a Mettler Toledo SevenCompact S210 pH meter with an InLab Routine Pro-ISM probe at 23 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of 19F pH sensor 1 for pH detection
ce:section.ce:para: To a solution of 3-amino-4-fluorophenol (1.27 g, 10.0 mmol) in ethanol (15 mL) was added 5-iodo-1,1,1-trifluoropentane (2.54 g, 10.1 mmol), and the reaction mixture was stirred for 14 h under reflux condition. The reaction mixture was then cooled to room temperature and evaporated under reduced pressure. The crude product was dissolved in chloroform and filtered. The organic layer was concentrated under reduced pressure and purified by column chromatography (silica gel, hexane/ethyl acetate (v/v, 4:1) was used as the eluent) to yield pH sensor 1 (0.50 g, 20%) as a brownish oil. 1H NMR (400 MHz, CDCl3) δ 6.83–6.78 (dd, J = 11.2, 8.4 Hz, 1H), 6.25–6.23 (dd, J = 7.2, 3.2 Hz, 1H), 6.08–6.05 (dt, J = 8.8, 3.2 Hz, 1H), 3.16–3.13 (t, J = 6.8 Hz, 2H), 2.19–2.05 (m, 2H), 1.77–1.63 (m, 4H); 13C NMR (100 MHz, CDCl3) δ 152.59, 146.62 (d, J = 229.8 Hz), 136.84 (d, J = 14.3 Hz), 127.14 (d, J = 274.6 Hz), 114.83 (d, J = 20.0 Hz), 102.79 (d, J = 4.8 Hz), 100.09, 43.57, 33.64 (q, J = 28.6 Hz), 28.42, 19.73 (d, J = 2.9 Hz); 19F NMR (376 MHz, CDCl3) δ −66.24, −146.61; HRMS (EI+) m/z calculated for C11H13F4NO [M]+: 251.0933, found: 251.0931. Details of the NMR and MS spectra are provided in the Supplementary data.

The final pH sensor 1 and all analyte solutions were prepared prior to 19F NMR experiments. All the sampling procedures were conducted using GILSON PIPETMAN Classic pipettes (1000 and 200 μL). Wilmad high throughput, precision, and coaxial insert NMR tubes were used for all 19F NMR measurements. For each 19F NMR experiment, 10 vol% deuterium oxide (D2O) was used for locking. NMR spectra were processed using JEOL Delta v5.3.3. control software. All 19F NMR spectra were optimized using the following procedures: (i) phase control; (ii) baseline correction using the polynomial fitting method with an average point (12) and order value (3); and (iii) referencing procedures for the chemical shift value of the internal standard signal defined as 0 ppm for the user-friendly analysis. Details of the procedures and the NMR acquisition parameters are depicted in each subsection of the Supplementary data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and synthesis of pH sensor 1 based on intramolecular internal standard
ce:section.ce:para: The design of pH sensor 1 for pH detection using 19F NMR spectroscopy was determined by screening several fluorobenzene derivatives. To start with, an initial screening was performed on mono-functionalized fluorobenzene compounds, such as fluoroaniline and fluorophenol derivatives, in the pH range from 1.0 to 13.0 (Figs. S1 and S2). According to the results, measured by adding trifluoroacetic acid (TFA) as an external standard, the compounds were categorized into two main groups based on their pH-responsive moieties. First, the fluoroaniline derivatives exhibited a change in chemical shift, mainly in the acidic range. The discrimination ability for pH was superior in the order in which the amino group was substituted in the para > ortho > meta position of fluorobenzene. Next, fluorophenol derivatives showed a change in chemical shift, mainly in the basic range, with 4-fluorophenol > 3-fluorophenol exhibiting good pH discrimination. Unfortunately, 2-fluorophenol was unable to discriminate pH.

After the initial screening, two optimized candidates with both amino and hydroxyl pH-responsive groups were selected to measure the pH under strongly acidic and basic conditions based on one molecular structure. One candidate was 2-amino-5-fluorophenol, which combines 4-fluoroaniline in the acidic range with 3-fluorophenol in the basic range, and the other was 3-amino-4-fluorophenol, which combines 4-fluorophenol in the basic range with 2-fluoroaniline in the acidic range. Secondary screening of the two selected candidates was conducted to evaluate their ability to measure the pH under the desired acidic and basic conditions (Fig. 1 ). As shown in Fig. 1B, 2-amino-5-fluorophenol was still able to discriminate pH only in the acidic range, and in the basic range, the change tendency of the chemical shift showed a reversal with increasing pH. In contrast, 3-amino-4-fluorophenol could not only distinguish pH in the acidic and basic ranges, but the range of change (approximately 14 ppm) was also relatively large (Fig. 1C). Subsequently, to apply the internal standard strategy, a 5-iodo-1,1,1-trifluoropentane moiety was covalently attached to the amino group of 3-amino-4-fluorophenol. As a result, pH sensor 1 (4-fluoro-3-((5,5,5-trifluoropentyl)amino)phenol), containing a CF3 moiety as an internal standard, was successfully synthesized (Scheme S1). Furthermore, pH sensor 1 demonstrated moderate solubility (up to 7 mM; 10 vol% DMSO; 25 °C) in aqueous solutions, enabling the detection of pH in aqueous samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of the intramolecular internal standard in 19F NMR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The validity of an internal standard as a reference signal depends on its resistance to changes in the surrounding environment, such as buffer media and pH. For this reason, the evaluation of the introduced internal standard was performed by pH titration (pH 1.0 to 13.0) based on TFA, which served as the valid standard signal (Fig. S3). Based on TFA (−76.550 ppm), the deviation of the signal of the CF3 moiety (FI, denoting the internal standard fluorine) as an internal standard was approximately 44 ppb within the measured pH range. This deviation of 44 ppb is an acceptable error and even lower than the previous reported result [45]. Meanwhile, the signal of the aromatic fluorine moiety (FR, denoting the fluorine substituted in benzene) shifted upfield with increasing pH, similar to the previously measured results for 3-amino-4-fluorophenol. However, as previously mentioned, the different components and performance of various spectrometers as well as individual analytical protocols can affect the reproducibility of 19F NMR-based measurements [38]. In this point of view, the proposed internal standard signal of pH sensor 1 was further evaluated on different NMR spectrometers belonging to several institutions (Scheme 2 ). All samples were identically prepared under two main conditions (pH 1.0 & pH 13.0) and were individually measured in experiments using five different instruments. As a reference protocol for user-friendly analysis, the chemical shift value of the FI was defined as 0 ppm, and a comparative analysis was performed with the chemical shift value of the FR measured under the same pH conditions. The deviations in the FR chemical shift were 19 ppb at −71.251 ppm at pH 1.0 (Scheme 2A) and 17 ppb at −85.526 ppm at pH 13.0 (Scheme 2B). Then, each chemical shift obtained under the certain pH conditions was converted to pH values based on the standard curve (Fig. S5). At pH 1.0, the corresponding pH values were in the range of 1.000–1.011 with a deviation of 0.005, and at pH 13.0, pH values ranged from 12.929 to 12.981 with a deviation of 0.022 (Table S1). The results for each deviation are an acceptable level of error and demonstrate that the introduced internal standard signal can serve as a reference signal. Therefore, pH sensor 1 with the internal standard strategy is expected to offer consistent results for a given pH with improved reproducibility, regardless of which 19F NMR spectroscopy is used.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH titration of pH sensor 1 via 19F NMR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The validity The pH-discriminating ability of pH sensor 1 was analyzed through pH titration experiments using 19F NMR spectroscopy in the pH range of 1.0–13.0 (Fig. 2 ). The two pH-responsive substituents (secondary amine and hydroxyl groups) in the aromatic ring of pH sensor 1 are directly affected by protonation and deprotonation upon changes in pH. However, due to the fast acid-base equilibrium, the observed chemical shift is only a weighted average of the various individual forms [31]. The performance of pH sensor 1 was prominently affected by strong acidic conditions (pH 1.0 to 5.0), causing chemical shift of FR to change from −71.276 ppm to −79.233 ppm based on the FI signal. On the other hand, under basic conditions (pH 8.0 to 13.0), the FR chemical shift change of pH sensor 1 was observed from −79.365 ppm to −85.539 ppm. Unlike dramatic changes of pH in the acidic and basic range, the pH was less distinguished in the neutral range (pH 5.0 to 7.0) due to minimal chemical shift differences. In addition, the pK a values for each functional group of pH sensor 1 were calculated based on the titration experiments conducted using 19F NMR. Despite the infrequent use of the 19F NMR-based method for determining pK a values, this method provided reliable results [46]. As shown in Fig. S4, the chemical shift values for each pH changed very little in the repeated experiments, and Boltzmann fitting to the experimental data resulted in pK a values of 2.04 and 10.34, for acidic (pH 1.0 to 5.0) and basic (pH 8.0 to 13.0) ranges, respectively. Based on these results, pH sensor 1 has high sensitivity to strongly acidic and basic pH ranges because of the inherent pK a value of each functional group and can easily detect a specific pH with reproducible chemical shift values.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interference and reversibility test of pH sensor 1 via 19F NMR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The capability of pH sensor 1 to measure pH in strongly acidic and basic ranges is expected to be a potential advantage for the detection of pH in certain samples, such as industrial wastewater, which has implications for public health. Prior to using pH sensor 1 for pH measurements in real samples, it was necessary to evaluate various effects, such as the viscosity, the temperature, and the concentration of pH sensor 1, to detect pH accurately. At first, ethylene glycol was utilized to increase the viscosity of the sample. Three samples constituting different ratios of ethylene glycol (0, 10, and 20 vol%) were measured under pH 1.0 and 13.0 conditions. According to Fig. S6, there was an overall tendency for chemical shift values to move upfield with increasing viscosity under both two pH conditions measured. This trend was relatively stronger at the acidic condition (pH 1.0) than the basic condition (pH 13.0). Then, to confirm the influence of temperature, each sample under the certain pH conditions (pH 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 11.0, 12.0, and 13.0) constituting the standard curve was measured at 298.0, 303.0, and 313.0 K, and tendencies of the standard curve differed based on the pH ranges (Fig. S5). In the acidic pH range, each chemical shift value of pH sensor 1 under tested pH conditions (pH 1.0, 2.0, and 3.0) was shifted toward the upfield as the temperature increases, exhibiting similar slopes of the standard curve (Fig. S5A). Meanwhile, in the basic pH range, the slopes of the standard curve for measured pH conditions (pH 11.0, 12.0, and 13.0) were slightly increased (Fig. S5B). Finally, samples with different concentrations (2.50, 3.75, and 5.00 mM) of pH sensor 1 were measured at pH 1.0, 4.0, 9.0 and 13.0 to consider the effect of the concentration of the pH sensor 1. Based on these results, chemical shift values of pH sensor 1 were slightly shifted toward the upfield as the concentration increases under the strongly acidic and basic conditions (pH 1.0 and 13.0) (Fig. S7A). However, the magnitudes of the chemical shift change were quite small, on the order of several tens of ppb. Moreover, there was little change in chemical shift values with concentration changes even under mildly acidic and basic conditions (pH 4.0 and 9.0) (Fig. S7B).

Afterwards, evaluation of pH sensor 1 for detection of pH in the presence of potentially interfering ionic species was performed through 19F NMR experiments. To this end, two types of multi-ionic samples were tested: simulated cationic black water, which contains various heavy metal ions (Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Cr3+, Cd2+, Hg2+, and Ni2+) following the World Health Organization (WHO) recommendations [47], and tap water. The experiments were performed under specific pH conditions (pH 1.0, 3.0, 11.0, and 13.0), and the 19F NMR spectra of pH sensor 1 were measured for each sample. As depicted in Fig. S8, the presence of various ionic species had negligible effects on the 19F NMR spectra of pH sensor 1 at pH 1.0, 3.0, 11.0, and 13.0. However, when the pH values were converted from the corresponding standard curve (Fig. S5), the lower pH values than the adjusted pH value were obtained at pH 11.0 because the measured chemical shift values at pH 11.0 were relatively shifted toward downfield. This phenomenon at pH 11.0 implied that the pH sensor 1 could be slightly affected by some paramagnetic ions under the specific pH condition. Nevertheless, in other pH conditions (pH 1.0, 3.0, and 13.0), calculated pH values of samples were mostly closed to adjusted pH values with the low absolute differences of 0.010–0.033 and 0.017−0.027 for cationic black water and tap water, respectively (Table 1 , Tables S2 and S3).

Next, the reversibility of pH sensor 1 according to pH change was investigated through 19F NMR experiments. As shown in Fig. S9, the chemical shift of FR appeared at a specific position as the pH changed between pH 1.0 and 13.0, and no new signals were observed during the rapid pH change. Interestingly, when the pH of the sample was adjusted to acidic pH 1.0 through dropwise addition of HCl solution, the chemical shift of FR, which was observed at pH 13.0, immediately shifted downfield. Conversely, using the NaOH solution, the chemical shift of FR, which was found at pH 13.0, appeared again. The chemical shift values at each pH value obtained through at least 3 cycles of this repeated process were extracted as the pH values from the corresponding standard curve (Fig. S5). The converted pH values exhibited a high degree of consistency for the measured conditions (Fig. S10). These results demonstrate the reliable pH detection capability and fast reversibility of pH sensor 1 in complex environments where rapid pH changes or various ions exist.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Applications in acidic beverages via 19F NMR spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: As the proposed pH sensor 1 has excellent pH detection capability for strongly acidic and basic ranges, its applicability to real samples was explored. pH measurements of diet Coke and lemonade among various acidic beverages were carried out using 19F NMR spectroscopy. The results, depicted in Fig. S11, demonstrate that pH sensor 1 effectively exhibits only signals for the internal standard (FI) and pH-dependent FR without any interference within each sample containing real acidic beverages. Furthermore, each pH value was obtained through the conversion of the chemical shift values of FR from the standard curve (Fig. S5) and showed a low absolute difference of 0.026–0.086 when compared to the pH values measured by the pH meter (Table 2 ). Unlike optical sensors, which are vulnerable to interference factors, such as the color of the sample, pH sensor 1 provided reliable pH values for a variety of acidic samples, even including dark-colored diet Coke. Therefore, this sensing platform based on the internal standard strategy can be used as a promising alternative for pH measurement in a variety of acidic samples.

In this study, an intramolecular internal standard strategy was applied to the development of a pH sensor using 19F NMR as a feasible alternative to the inherent limitations in the reproducibility of 19F NMR-based techniques. Informed by 19F NMR-based screening, an initial investigation of six mono-functionalized fluorobenzenes and two di-functionalized fluorobenzenes, a 3-amino-4-fluorophenol moiety was identified that exhibited good pH sensitivity under strongly acidic and basic conditions. By covalently introducing another fluorine moiety as an internal standard to the selected fluorinated compound, the pH sensor 1 was synthesized for pH detection. The internal standard signal, which was evaluated using a valid external standard material (TFA), showed a consistent response to pH changes with a low deviation (for chemical shift/pH value = 44 ppb/0.010). Notably, as confirmed by the individual results from several institutions, the bias (for chemical shift/pH value = 19 ppb/0.005 at pH 1.0; 17 ppb/0.022 at pH 13.0) based on the internal standard signal of pH sensor 1 was very low, demonstrating improved reproducibility. Based on the proposed internal standard strategy, the results of pH titration of pH sensor 1 via 19F NMR spectroscopy showed that it could effectively detect pH in the acidic and basic ranges, exhibiting pK a values of 2.04 and 10.34. Furthermore, its 19F NMR response was highly stable in samples with various ions as potential factors of interference, even in those containing several heavy metals associated with industrial wastewater. In addition, the pH detection platform successfully performed pH measurements over rapid pH changes with high reversibility. Finally, pH measurements using 19F NMR spectroscopy in complex media, such as diet Coke and lemonade, provided low absolute differences when it was compared to pH values obtained from the pH meter. Overall, we anticipate that this simple pH detection system with an internal standard strategy in 19F NMR has great potential for use in a wide range of acidic samples for which pH measurement is required.
Mincheol Jang: Methodology, Investigation, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Min Su Han: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) grant funded by the Korea government (MSIT) (RS-2023-00247332 and RS-2023-00220174).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2023.341558.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dysregulated pH: a perfect storm for cancer progression

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B.A. Webb, M. Chimenti, M.P. Jacobson, D.L. Barber, Dysregulated pH: a perfect storm for cancer progression, Nat. Rev. Cancer 11 (2011) 671-677.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in electrochemical pH-sensing materials and configurations for biomedical applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.T. Ghoneim, A. Nguyen, N. Dereje, J. Huang, G.C. Moore, P.J. Murzynowski, C. Dagdeviren, Recent progress in electrochemical pH-sensing materials and configurations for biomedical applications, Chem. Rev. 119 (2019) 5248-5297.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Assessment of natural groundwater physico-chemical properties in major industrial and residential locations of Lagos metropolis

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.T. Popoola, A.S. Yusuff, T.A. Aderibigbe, Assessment of natural groundwater physico-chemical properties in major industrial and residential locations of Lagos metropolis, Appl. Water Sci. 9 (2019) 191.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impact of various acids and bases on the voltammetric response of platinum group metal oxides

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Chalupczok, P. Kurzweil, H. Hartmann, Impact of various acids and bases on the voltammetric response of platinum group metal oxides, Int. J. Electrochem. 2018 (2018) 1-6.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Wastewater treatment by means of advanced oxidation processes at basic pH conditions: a review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Boczkaj, A. Fernandes, Wastewater treatment by means of advanced oxidation processes at basic pH conditions: A review, Chem. Eng. J. 320 (2017) 608-633.
